bibliographic_data.title: Coupled-wave model for square-lattice photonic-crystal lasers with TE polarization -- a general approach
bibliographic_data.abstract: A general coupled-wave model is presented for square-lattice photonic crystal (PC) lasers with transverse-electric polarization. This model incorporates the high-order coupling effects that are important for two-dimensional PC laser cavities and gives a general and rigorous coupled-wave formulation for the full three-dimensional structures of typical laser devices. Numerical examples based on our model are presented for PC structures with different air-hole shapes. The accuracy of the results obtained is verified using three-dimensional finite-difference time-domain simulations.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a general coupled-wave model for square-lattice photonic crystal lasers with TE polarization, incorporating high-order coupling effects and providing accurate results through 3D simulations.
core_content.key_methodology: A rigorous coupled-wave formulation is developed by extending the approach from Streifer et al. to include more wavevectors in 3D systems, applicable to arbitrary air-hole shapes.
outcomes: Developed a general coupled-wave model for square-lattice photonic crystal lasers with TE polarization
Incorporated high-order coupling effects and accurately modeled three-dimensional structures
Verified results using 3D finite-difference time-domain simulations
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper presents a novel approach to modeling square-lattice photonic crystal (PC) lasers with transverse-electric polarization. The authors develop a general coupled-wave model that incorporates high-order coupling effects, which are crucial for two-dimensional PC laser cavities. This model is extended from the one-dimensional distributed feedback (DFB) lasers by Streifer et al., to include more wavevectors in three-dimensional systems and apply it to arbitrary air-hole shapes. Numerical examples based on this model are provided for different air-hole shapes, and the accuracy of the results is confirmed through 3D finite-difference time-domain simulations.
